title: Power Derating Practice
learningObjectives: Derate power to keep components cool under stress
Use resistance to cut power without losing circuit validity
Relate power changes to both voltage and current
introText: Derating keeps components reliable. You'll start near a component limit, then reduce voltage and increase resistance to create a safe margin.

steps.instructionText: Place a 12V source and a resistor in series and wire the loop closed.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Loop built — you can measure power now.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Connect the source and resistor end-to-end so the circuit is valid.

steps.instructionText: Start with a low resistor value so power is near 0.5 W, then raise resistance until power falls below 0.25 W.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Power is under 0.25 W — safely derated.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Increase resistance or reduce voltage until power is below 0.25 W.

steps.instructionText: Confirm current is also under 25 mA after derating.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Current is under 25 mA, aligning with the new power budget.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Increase resistance further or lower voltage to bring current below 25 mA.

questions.questionText: Why derate power below a component's maximum?
options: To extend lifetime and avoid overheating
questions.explanation: Running below the max rating keeps parts cooler and more reliable.

questions.questionText: Which change lowers power the most efficiently?
questions.explanation: Lowering voltage and/or raising resistance reduces both current and power.
